title: Reading and Use of English – Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about the history of the Everest expeditions. Choose from paragraphs A–G which fits each gap. There is one extra paragraph.

content.articleTitle: The Roof of the World: Conquering Everest
content.text: Mount Everest, or Chomolungma as it is known to the local Sherpa people, has always exerted a powerful pull on the human imagination. At 8,848 meters, it is the highest point on Earth, a place where the air is too thin to sustain life and the weather can change from calm to deadly in minutes. The quest to reach the summit began in earnest in the 1920s. ||(41)|| These early British expeditions were massive logistical undertakings, involving hundreds of porters carrying tons of equipment across the Tibetan plateau. However, the climbers faced a wall of ice and wind that proved insurmountable. The most famous of these early attempts was the 1924 mission, where George Mallory and Andrew Irvine vanished into the mists near the summit. ||(42)|| Following World War II, the political map of the region changed. Tibet closed its borders, but Nepal opened its doors to Westerners for the first time. This allowed for a new approach from the south, leading to the legendary 1953 expedition led by John Hunt. ||(43)|| On May 29, 1953, Edmund Hillary and Tenzing Norgay finally stood where no human had stood before. Their success was not just a feat of physical endurance, but a triumph of coordination and the use of new technology, specifically improved bottled oxygen. ||(44)|| In the decades that followed, the nature of Everest climbing transformed. What was once the domain of elite explorers became a goal for commercial expeditions. By the 1990s, companies were offering to guide paying clients to the top, regardless of their previous high-altitude experience. ||(45)|| The 1996 disaster, in which eight people died in a single day, highlighted the dangers of this 'tourist' approach. Critics argued that the overcrowding of the mountain was leading to dangerous bottlenecks in the 'Death Zone,' where every minute spent waiting significantly increases the risk of frostbite and exhaustion. ||(46)|| Today, the mountain faces a new crisis of waste and environmental degradation. Tons of abandoned tents, oxygen bottles, and human waste litter the high camps, earning Everest the unfortunate nickname of 'the world's highest junkyard.' The challenge for the future is not just how to climb the mountain, but how to protect it for generations to come.

paragraphs.A: Indeed, [41] the initial motivation [/41] was partly national pride. Having lost the race to the North and South Poles, the British Empire saw the 'Third Pole' as the last great prize of exploration, investing heavily in the hopes of being the first to the top.
paragraphs.B: Whether [42] they actually reached the peak [/42] remains one of the greatest mysteries in mountaineering history. If they did, it would mean the mountain was conquered 29 years earlier than officially recorded, though most experts believe the technical difficulties of the 'Second Step' would have been too great for their 1920s gear.
paragraphs.C: This [43] shift in geography [/43] meant that climbers now had to tackle the Khumbu Icefall, a terrifying, shifting labyrinth of ice towers. It was a daunting prospect, but it also provided a more direct route to the South Col and the final ridge leading to the summit.
paragraphs.D: This [44] breakthrough in life-support [/44] allowed the pair to maintain their strength in the thin air. When the news of their achievement reached London on the morning of Queen Elizabeth II's coronation, it was seen as a glorious omen for a new era of British history.
paragraphs.E: This [45] commercialization of the peak [/45] brought a surge in numbers, but it also brought a change in the demographic of the climbers. Success was now often measured by the ability to pay for a spot on a team rather than the years of training required to master the craft of alpinism.
paragraphs.F: This [46] tragic turn of events [/46] served as a wake-up call for the climbing community. It led to stricter regulations and a renewed debate about the ethics of 'adventure tourism' in environments where nature remains a fundamentally untamable force.
paragraphs.G: Many [47] Sherpas now refuse [/47] to work for low-budget teams. They argue that the lack of proper support not only endangers the clients but also puts the lives of the local guides at unnecessary risk, leading to a push for higher safety standards across the industry.

questions.explanation: A explains the 'initial motivation' (national pride) for the British expeditions mentioned in the text following the gap.
B refers to the mystery of whether Mallory and Irvine 'reached the peak', following the mention of their disappearance in 1924.
C links the 'shift in geography' (moving from Tibet to Nepal) to the new challenges of the Khumbu Icefall.
D identifies the 'breakthrough in life-support' (bottled oxygen) used by Hillary and Tenzing during their successful 1953 summit.
E describes the 'commercialization of the peak' and the change in climber demographics, following the mention of paying clients.
F refers to the 'tragic turn of events' (the 1996 disaster) and the resulting 'wake-up call' for the climbing community.
